concept_name: Field of an Infinite Line Charge: E = λ/(2πε₀r), a 1/r falloff
source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Ch.1 Electric Charges and Fields (field of an infinite straight line charge, §1.15 applications of Gauss's law) — table-of-contents + Indian-anchor reference only; teaching authored from first principles. HC Verma consulted for SEQUENCE (we already KNOW Gauss's law Φ = q_enc/ε₀, but that is total flux, not the field at a point → an infinite uniform line has cylindrical symmetry, so by symmetry E must be radial, perpendicular to the line, and equal in magnitude on a coaxial ring at distance r → that constant-and-perpendicular E is exactly what lets E come out of the flux integral → choose a COAXIAL GAUSSIAN CYLINDER of radius r and height L: the two flat end caps carry zero flux because E grazes them (E·dA = 0), so only the curved wall counts, Φ = E·(2πrL) → enclosed charge on a length L is q_enc = λL, so Gauss gives Φ = λL/ε₀ → set them equal: E·(2πrL) = λL/ε₀, the L cancels, leaving E = λ/(2πε₀r) → finally compare with a point charge: the line falls off as 1/r, NOT 1/r², because the cylinder area 2πrL grows only linearly with r while a sphere's 4πr² grows quadratically (PRIMARY aha)), never copied. The atomic idea: applying Gauss's law plus cylindrical symmetry to an infinite uniform line of linear charge density λ gives a purely radial field, perpendicular to the line and independent of axial position, E = λ/(2πε₀r) — a field that falls off as 1/r (slower than a point charge's 1/r²). The two flat end caps of the coaxial Gaussian cylinder carry exactly zero flux; the cylinder height L cancels, so E depends only on λ and the perpendicular distance r, never on the length of wire enclosed. SCOPE: the infinite LINE / WIRE only — does NOT re-teach the Gauss statement Φ = q_enc/ε₀ (gauss_law), the open-surface flux definition Φ = E·A (electric_flux), the meaning of λ itself (charge_distribution), the spherical-shell / solid-sphere cases (gauss_law_sphere / gauss_law_solid_sphere), the infinite-plane/sheet case, or finite-line end effects. NEW field_3d gauss_law_line scenario: an infinite vertical line of charge with λ + markers, a coaxial Gaussian cylinder (curved wall + two end caps) of adjustable radius r and height L, a horizontal ring of equal radial wall arrows (1/r length, same all round), grazing end-cap arrows with Φ=0 tags, two distinct reference lines (r perpendicular billboarded camera-right, L axial), a derivation write-in where the L visibly cancels, a STATE_6 E-vs-r plot showing the line's 1/r curve above the faint point-charge 1/r² ghost with a coordinated r-sweep, and a STATE_7 λ + r slider pair with stable explorer ID gauss_line_explorer (Rule 27). Prerequisites gauss_law + electric_flux + charge_distribution. Conceptual-only (no mode_overrides), EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; misconceptions confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a). Third Gauss-application sibling after gauss_law_sphere (1/r²) and gauss_law_solid_sphere (ramp-then-1/r²); this is the cylindrical-symmetry / 1/r member.

real_world_anchor.primary: A high-tension overhead transmission line strung between steel towers, running for kilometres across the countryside — a single straight conductor carrying charge spread evenly along its length. To a probe held a few metres away, that line is effectively infinitely long and uniformly charged. The reason engineers can predict the field near it with one clean formula, E = λ/(2πε₀r), is cylindrical symmetry plus Gauss's law: the field points straight out from the wire and weakens as 1/r — slower than the 1/r² field of a single point charge.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A long thin charged metal rod in the lab, or the tall charged column of a Van de Graaff generator. Near the middle, well away from the ends, the field points radially straight out from the rod and dies away as 1/r. Walk twice as far from the wire and the field only halves — not quarters, the way it would around a single charged ball.

lambda.name: Linear charge density on the wire

r.name: Perpendicular distance from the line to the field point (= Gaussian-cylinder radius)

L.name: Axial height of the Gaussian cylinder (a length of wire; cancels out of E)

epsilon_0.name: Permittivity of free space (a fixed constant of nature)

DEMO_E_PER_NC.name: Demo field readout per nanocoulomb-per-metre, at unit demo radius

constraints: E is strictly radial and perpendicular to the line everywhere (E parallel r-hat, r measured perpendicular from the line) (C1).
|E| is constant at every point of a coaxial ring at perpendicular distance r - this is what lets E leave the flux integral (C2).
E is independent of position ALONG the line (axial position) and independent of the cylinder height L: the field at a point depends only on lambda and the perpendicular distance r (C3).
The two flat end caps carry exactly zero flux: E is radial, so it lies in the plane of each cap (E.dA = 0); only the curved wall carries flux (C4).
Total flux equals wall flux: Phi = E*(2*pi*r*L) = q_enc/epsilon_0 = lambda*L/epsilon_0; the L cancels, giving E = lambda/(2*pi*epsilon_0*r) (C5).
E falls off as 1/r (NOT 1/r^2): doubling r halves E. This is slower than a point charge 1/r^2 (doubling r quarters E) (C6).
The 1/r law follows because the Gaussian cylinder curved area 2*pi*r*L grows LINEARLY with r, while a sphere 4*pi*r^2 grows QUADRATICALLY (C7).
Arrow direction follows the sign of lambda: outward for +lambda, inward for -lambda; magnitude scales linearly with |lambda| at fixed r (C8).

aha_moment.statement: A line charge field falls off as 1/r, not 1/r² — slower than a point.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: The wire's 1/r curve draws in above the faint 1/r² point-charge ghost — same start at r=1, then dying away slower — while the coordinated r-sweep shrinks the ring arrows as 1/r in lockstep with the tracking dot.

STATE_1.title: A long charged wire — how does E fall off with r?

scene_composition.text: a long wire, charge λ spread evenly along it
walk away by r — how does E weaken?
E ∝ 1/r?   E ∝ 1/r²?   your first guess

tts_sentences.text_en: Picture a high-tension power line strung across the countryside, a single straight wire running for kilometres, carrying charge spread evenly along it.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक high-tension power line की कल्पना करो जो countryside के पार फैली है — एक single straight wire जो kilometres तक चलती है, जिस पर charge evenly spread है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక high-tension power line ని imagine చేయండి — countryside అంతటా వ్యాపించిన ఒక single straight wire, kilometres పొడవు నడుస్తూ, దానిపై charge evenly spread అయి ఉంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: As you walk away, the field weakens with distance r. It's tempting to expect the point-charge law, one over r squared — but we'll derive its real law.
tts_sentences.text_hi: जब आप दूर जाते हो, field distance r के साथ कमजोर होता है। Point-charge law, one over r squared, expect करना tempting है — लेकिन हम इसका real law derive करेंगे।
tts_sentences.text_te: మీరు దూరంగా వెళ్ళగా, field distance r తో తగ్గుతుంది. Point-charge law, one over r squared, expect చేయడం tempting — కానీ మనం దీని real law ని derive చేస్తాం.

misconception_watch.belief: The field of a charged wire must fall off as 1/r^2, the same way a point charge does.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The hook shows only the wire and its charge markers - no field law yet; the guess is held until STATE_6 reveals the wire curve sitting ABOVE the point-charge curve.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A line is not a point: its field falls off as 1/r, slower than a point charge 1/r^2 - proven at STATE_6.

STATE_2.title: Same strength all around: the field is purely radial

scene_composition.text: field point P, one step out at distance r
along the wire, toward it, or straight out?
E ∥ r̂,  same |E| all around the ring at r

tts_sentences.text_en: Point at P, a step out from the wire. The field there could push three ways: along the wire, toward the nearest bit, or straight out.
tts_sentences.text_hi: P पर ध्यान दो, wire से एक कदम बाहर। Field वहाँ तीन तरह से push कर सकता है: wire के along, nearest bit की तरफ, या straight out।
tts_sentences.text_te: P పైన చూడండి, wire నుండి ఒక అడుగు బయట. Field అక్కడ మూడు విధాలుగా push చేయవచ్చు: wire along గా, nearest bit వైపు, లేదా straight out గా.

tts_sentences.text_en: The wire looks identical from every direction, so the field can only point straight out, at right angles to the line.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Wire हर direction से identical दिखती है, इसलिए field केवल straight out, line के right angles पर ही point कर सकता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Wire అన్ని directions నుండి identical గా కనిపిస్తుంది, కాబట్టి field మాత్రమే straight out గా, line కి right angles లో point చేయగలదు.

tts_sentences.text_en: And every arrow on that ring has the same length, same strength all the way around, at this distance r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: और उस ring पर हर arrow की same length है — इस distance r पर, पूरे रास्ते same strength।
tts_sentences.text_te: మరియు ఆ ring పై ప్రతి arrow కి same length ఉంది — ఈ distance r వద్ద, అన్ని వైపులా same strength.

misconception_watch.belief: The field points along the wire, or leans toward the nearest bit of wire.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A full ring of equal-length arrows points straight out, perpendicular to the line; rotating the wire about its axis leaves the picture unchanged.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Cylindrical symmetry forces E to be radial and equal in magnitude all around a coaxial ring at distance r.

STATE_3.title: Choose a coaxial Gaussian CYLINDER (radius r, height L)

scene_composition.text: wrap ONE closed surface, not sum every dq
sphere, or cylinder — which matches?
curved wall area = 2πr·L

tts_sentences.text_en: We could add Coulomb's law from every tiny piece of charge, but that's slow. Instead we wrap one closed surface around the wire — and its shape must match the wire.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हम Coulomb's law को हर tiny piece से add कर सकते हैं, लेकिन वो slow है। इसके बजाय हम wire के चारों तरफ एक closed surface wrap करते हैं — और उसकी shape wire से match होनी चाहिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: మనం Coulomb's law ని ప్రతి tiny piece నుండి add చేయవచ్చు, కానీ అది slow. దానికి బదులు wire చుట్టూ ఒక closed surface wrap చేస్తాం — దాని shape wire తో match అవ్వాలి.

tts_sentences.text_en: A cylinder, coaxial with the wire, because it matches the wire's shape. On its curved wall the field is constant and points straight through.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Wire के साथ coaxial एक cylinder, क्योंकि यह wire की shape से match करता है। इसकी curved wall पर field constant है और straight through point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Wire తో coaxial గా ఉన్న ఒక cylinder, ఎందుకంటే అది wire యొక్క shape తో match అవుతుంది. దాని curved wall పై field constant గా ఉంటుంది మరియు straight through point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Give the cylinder a height L. The flux through its curved wall is the field times the wall area, two pi r times L.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Cylinder को एक height L दो। इसकी curved wall से होकर जाने वाला flux है field times wall area, two pi r times L।
tts_sentences.text_te: Cylinder కి ఒక height L ఇవ్వండి. దాని curved wall ద్వారా వెళ్ళే flux అంటే field times wall area, two pi r times L.

misconception_watch.belief: You must add up Coulomb's law from every tiny piece of charge to get the field.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: One coaxial Gaussian cylinder replaces the piece-by-piece sum; on its curved wall E is constant, so the flux is just E times the wall area.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Gauss plus cylindrical symmetry replaces the dq-by-dq Coulomb integral with one closed surface and one equation.

STATE_4.title: The two flat end caps carry ZERO flux

scene_composition.text: curved wall + two flat end caps
Φ = 0 on each cap (E grazes it)
Φ = Φ_wall = E·(2πr·L)

tts_sentences.text_en: The cylinder has three pieces: the curved wall and two flat end caps. The field pierces only some of them.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Cylinder के तीन हिस्से हैं: curved wall और दो flat end caps। Field उनमें से केवल कुछ को ही pierce करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Cylinder లో మూడు భాగాలు: curved wall మరియు రెండు flat end caps. Field వాటిలో కొన్నింటిని మాత్రమే pierce చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: The field is radial, so on each flat cap it just grazes along the surface, it never pokes through. Zero flux on both caps.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Field radial है, इसलिए हर flat cap पर वो बस surface के साथ graze करता है, कभी through नहीं पोकता। दोनों caps पर zero flux।
tts_sentences.text_te: Field radial గా ఉంటుంది, కాబట్టి ప్రతి flat cap పై అది surface వెంట graze చేస్తుంది, ఎప్పుడూ through పోకుండా. రెండు caps పై zero flux.

tts_sentences.text_en: Only the curved wall is pierced. So the whole flux is the wall flux, E times two pi r L.
tts_sentences.text_hi: केवल curved wall ही pierce होती है। तो पूरा flux है wall flux — E times two pi r L।
tts_sentences.text_te: కేవలం curved wall మాత్రమే pierce అవుతుంది. కాబట్టి మొత్తం flux అంటే wall flux — E times two pi r L.

misconception_watch.belief: The two flat end caps carry flux too, so they belong in the calculation.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Arrows graze along each flat cap (Phi = 0 tag) while piercing the curved wall; only the wall flux survives.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: E is radial, so it lies flat in each end cap (E dot dA = 0); only the curved wall carries flux, area 2 pi r L.

STATE_5.title: Φ = E·(2πrL) = λL/ε₀ ⇒ E = λ/(2πε₀r); L cancels

scene_composition.text: q_enc = λL  ⇒  Φ = λL/ε₀
E·(2πrL) = λL/ε₀ — watch L
E = λ/(2πε₀r)   (no L left)

tts_sentences.text_en: How much charge sits inside the cylinder? Just the charge on a length L of wire, that is lambda times L. By Gauss, the flux equals lambda-L over epsilon-nought.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Cylinder के अंदर कितना charge है? बस wire की length L पर जो charge है, यानी lambda times L। Gauss के अनुसार, flux equals lambda-L over epsilon-nought।
tts_sentences.text_te: Cylinder లో ఎంత charge ఉంది? కేవలం wire యొక్క length L పై ఉన్న charge, అంటే lambda times L. Gauss ప్రకారం, flux equals lambda-L over epsilon-nought.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now we have two expressions for the same flux. Set them equal and solve for the field E, and watch what happens to the length L.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब हमारे पास same flux के लिए दो expressions हैं। उन्हें equal set करो और field E के लिए solve करो — और देखो length L के साथ क्या होता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మన దగ్గర same flux కోసం రెండు expressions ఉన్నాయి. వాటిని equal గా set చేసి field E కోసం solve చేయండి — మరియు length L తో ఏమి జరుగుతుందో చూడండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: The length L on each side cancels. The field is lambda over two pi epsilon-nought r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हर side का length L cancel हो जाता है। Field है lambda over two pi epsilon-nought r।
tts_sentences.text_te: ప్రతి side యొక్క length L cancel అయిపోతుంది. Field అంటే lambda over two pi epsilon-nought r.

tts_sentences.text_en: The length L is gone, so it never mattered how long a piece of wire you wrapped. The field depends only on lambda and your distance r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Length L चली गई, तो आपने wire का कितना टुकड़ा wrap किया वो कभी matter नहीं करता। Field केवल lambda और आपकी distance r पर depend करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Length L పోయింది, కాబట్టి మీరు wire యొక్క ఎంత పొడవు wrap చేశారు అనేది ఎప్పుడూ matter అవ్వలేదు. Field మాత్రమే lambda మరియు మీ distance r పై depend చేస్తుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: A longer cylinder, or a longer piece of wire, must change the field you compute.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The write-in strikes the L on both sides; the solved field E = lambda/(2 pi epsilon_0 r) has no L in it at all.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The L cancels: enclosed charge lambda*L and wall area 2 pi r L both scale with L, so E never depends on the length you wrap.

STATE_6.title: 1/r, NOT 1/r²: the line falls off slower than a point charge

scene_composition.text: point charge: E ∝ 1/r² (faint ghost)
the wire: E ∝ 1/r — dies away slower
2πr·L grows linearly  vs  4πr² grows as r²

tts_sentences.text_en: You already know a point charge fades as one over r squared. Here is that curve, drawn faint.
tts_sentences.text_hi: आप पहले से जानते हो कि एक point charge one over r squared की तरह fade होता है। यह रहा वो curve, faint में drawn।
tts_sentences.text_te: మీకు ఇప్పటికే తెలుసు — ఒక point charge one over r squared లా fade అవుతుంది. ఇదిగో ఆ curve, faint గా drawn చేయబడింది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Back at the start, the instinct was that the wire fades the same way. It does not — watch the wire's own curve as the distance r grows.
tts_sentences.text_hi: शुरुआत में instinct यह था कि wire उसी तरह fade होती है। ऐसा नहीं है — wire के अपने curve को देखो जैसे-जैसे distance r बढ़ता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: మొదట్లో instinct ఏమిటంటే wire అదే విధంగా fade అవుతుందని. అలా జరగదు — distance r పెరిగే కొద్దీ wire యొక్క curve ని చూడండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: The wire's curve sits above the point charge's, same start at r equals one, but it dies away slower, as one over r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Wire का curve point charge के ऊपर बैठता है, r equals one पर same start, लेकिन यह slower fade होता है — one over r की तरह।
tts_sentences.text_te: Wire యొక్క curve point charge దానికంటే పైన ఉంటుంది, r equals one వద్ద same start, కానీ అది slower గా fade అవుతుంది — one over r లా.

tts_sentences.text_en: Why slower? The cylinder area, two pi r L, grows straight-line with the distance r, while a sphere's four pi r squared grows much faster.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Slower क्यों? Cylinder area, two pi r L, distance r के साथ straight-line में बढ़ता है, जबकि sphere का four pi r squared बहुत faster बढ़ता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Slower ఎందుకు? Cylinder area, two pi r L, distance r తో straight-line లో పెరుగుతుంది, అయితే sphere యొక్క four pi r squared చాలా faster గా పెరుగుతుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: The wire field falls off as 1/r^2, like a point charge - doubling r quarters the field.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The wire's 1/r curve sits above the faint 1/r^2 ghost; doubling r only HALVES the wire field, while the cylinder area 2 pi r L grows merely linearly.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The line falls off as 1/r (doubling r halves E), slower than a point 1/r^2 (doubling r quarters E).

STATE_7.title: Explore: tune λ and r, watch the arrows rescale as 1/r

scene_composition.text: push λ up → every ring arrow grows together
drag r out → arrows shrink as 1/r
E depends only on λ and r — never on where along the wire

tts_sentences.text_en: Now you drive. Push lambda up, every arrow on the ring grows together, and the readout climbs.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब आप drive करो। Lambda ऊपर push करो, ring पर हर arrow एक साथ बढ़ता है, और readout चढ़ता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మీరు drive చేయండి. Lambda ని పైకి push చేయండి — ring పై ప్రతి arrow కలిసి పెరుగుతుంది, మరియు readout పైకి వెళ్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Drag the radius r outward, the arrows shrink as one over r, and the readout slides down along the curve.
tts_sentences.text_hi: radius r को outward drag करो, arrows one over r की तरह shrink होते हैं, और readout curve के साथ नीचे slide करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: radius r ని outward గా drag చేయండి — arrows one over r లా shrink అవుతాయి, మరియు readout curve వెంట కిందికి slide అవుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Notice the field E never cares where along the wire you stand, only lambda and your perpendicular distance r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: गौर करो कि field E को कभी फर्क नहीं पड़ता कि आप wire के कहाँ खड़े हो — केवल lambda और आपकी perpendicular distance r।
tts_sentences.text_te: గమనించండి — field E కి మీరు wire వెంట ఎక్కడ నిల్చున్నారో పట్టదు, కేవలం lambda మరియు మీ perpendicular distance r మాత్రమే.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered the field of an infinite line charge when they can (1) state E = lambda/(2*pi*epsilon_0*r), radial and perpendicular to the line; (2) explain that cylindrical symmetry makes E constant and perpendicular on the curved wall so Phi = E*(2*pi*r*L) = q_enc/epsilon_0; (3) show that the two flat end caps carry zero flux because E is parallel to them; (4) show that L cancels, so E depends only on lambda and the perpendicular distance r, never on the length of wire enclosed or on axial position; and (5) recognise that E falls off as 1/r, slower than a point charge 1/r^2, because the cylinder area 2*pi*r*L grows linearly with r.

questions.stem: An infinite straight wire carries uniform linear charge density lambda. What is the electric field at a perpendicular distance r from the wire?

options.A: E = lambda/(2*pi*epsilon_0*r) - radial, perpendicular to the wire
options.B: E = lambda/(4*pi*epsilon_0*r^2) - falling off as 1/r^2 like a point charge
options.C: E = lambda/(2*pi*epsilon_0*r^2) - the line version of the inverse-square law
options.D: E = q/(4*pi*epsilon_0*r^2) with q the total charge on the wire

distractor_misconceptions.B: applies a point charge 1/r^2 falloff to a line, halving the correct power of r
distractor_misconceptions.C: keeps the correct 2*pi prefactor but wrongly uses 1/r^2 instead of 1/r
distractor_misconceptions.D: treats the infinite wire as a single point charge with a finite total charge q

questions.tested_idea: The field of an infinite line charge is E = lambda/(2*pi*epsilon_0*r), radial and 1/r.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A long high-tension power line carries charge lambda per metre. Write the electric field a probe feels at perpendicular distance r, away from the line ends.

questions.stem: At a point P beside an infinite charged wire, in what direction does the electric field point?

options.A: Radially outward, perpendicular to the wire - straight away from the line
options.B: Along the wire, parallel to its length
options.C: Toward the nearest single point of the wire, at a slant
options.D: It spirals around the wire like a magnetic field

distractor_misconceptions.B: thinks the field runs along the wire rather than out from it
distractor_misconceptions.C: imagines the field aiming at the closest bit of wire instead of being purely radial
distractor_misconceptions.D: confuses the electric field of a line charge with the circular magnetic field of a current

questions.tested_idea: Cylindrical symmetry makes E purely radial and perpendicular to the line.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Hold a tiny positive test charge near a long charged rod. Which way does the force on it point relative to the rod?

questions.stem: A coaxial Gaussian cylinder (radius r, height L) is drawn around an infinite charged wire. Through which part of its surface does the electric flux pass?

options.A: Only the curved side wall; the two flat end caps carry zero flux
options.B: Equally through the curved wall and both end caps
options.C: Only through the two end caps; the curved wall carries none
options.D: Through all three surfaces, but mostly through the nearer end cap

distractor_misconceptions.B: thinks every face of a closed surface must carry flux
distractor_misconceptions.C: inverts which surface the radial field actually pierces
distractor_misconceptions.D: imagines the field leaking out of the ends of the cylinder

questions.tested_idea: E is radial, so it grazes the flat caps (E dot dA = 0); only the curved wall carries flux.
questions.parallel_form_stem: You wrap a soup-can-shaped Gaussian surface around a charged wire. Which faces of the can does the field actually pass through?

questions.stem: How does the field of an infinite line charge fall off with distance, compared with a point charge?

options.A: The line falls off as 1/r, slower than the point charge 1/r^2
options.B: Both fall off as 1/r^2 - a line is just many point charges in a row
options.C: The line falls off as 1/r^2 and the point charge as 1/r
options.D: The line field does not weaken with distance at all

distractor_misconceptions.B: assumes summing point charges keeps the 1/r^2 law instead of giving 1/r
distractor_misconceptions.C: swaps the two falloff laws between line and point
distractor_misconceptions.D: thinks an infinite line gives a uniform field that never weakens

questions.tested_idea: The line falls off as 1/r (slower) because the cylinder area 2*pi*r*L grows linearly, not as r^2.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Walking away from a long charged power line versus away from a single charged ball - whose field dies away faster, and what power of r does each follow?

questions.stem: A probe at distance r1 from an infinite charged wire feels field E1. It is moved to 2*r1. What is the new field?

options.A: E1/2 - the line field halves when r doubles, because it goes as 1/r
options.B: E1/4 - the field quarters when r doubles, as for a point charge
options.C: E1 - the field is the same everywhere outside an infinite wire
options.D: 2*E1 - the field grows as you move the probe outward

distractor_misconceptions.B: applies the point charge 1/r^2 (quarter) instead of the line 1/r (half)
distractor_misconceptions.C: thinks an infinite wire gives a distance-independent field
distractor_misconceptions.D: believes the field increases with distance from the wire

questions.tested_idea: E goes as 1/r, so doubling r halves the field (vs quartering for a 1/r^2 point charge).
questions.parallel_form_stem: Near an HT power line a probe reads field E1 at 2 metres. What does it read at 4 metres from the line?

questions.stem: Two students wrap Gaussian cylinders of DIFFERENT heights (L and 3L) around the same charged wire to find E at the same distance r. What do they get?

options.A: The same E - the height L cancels, so E depends only on lambda and r
options.B: The taller cylinder gives three times the field, since it encloses more charge
options.C: The shorter cylinder gives a stronger field, since the charge is more concentrated
options.D: They cannot get E without first agreeing on a cylinder height

distractor_misconceptions.B: sees q_enc = lambda*L grow with L but forgets the wall area 2*pi*r*L grows the same way, cancelling
distractor_misconceptions.C: invents a concentration effect from a shorter cylinder
distractor_misconceptions.D: thinks the answer depends on the arbitrary length of wire enclosed

questions.tested_idea: Both q_enc and the wall area scale with L, so L cancels: E is independent of the enclosed length.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Two people draw soup-can Gaussian surfaces of different lengths around the same charged wire. Do they compute the same field at a given distance, or different ones?

STATE_1.label: A long charged wire — how does E fall off with r?

STATE_2.label: Same strength all around: the field is purely radial
STATE_2.formula_overlay: E ∥ r̂,  |E| constant on the ring at r

STATE_3.label: Choose a coaxial Gaussian CYLINDER (radius r, height L)

STATE_4.label: The two flat end caps carry ZERO flux

STATE_5.label: Φ = E·(2πrL) = λL/ε₀ ⇒ E = λ/(2πε₀r); L cancels

STATE_6.label: 1/r, NOT 1/r²: the line falls off slower than a point charge

STATE_7.label: Explore: tune λ and r, watch the arrows rescale as 1/r
